script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. Tuesday — here is your MEME recap. The portfolio closed six point one seven percent down on the session. Every primary holding finished in the red, and the damage was broad.

Sandisk dropped twelve point six three percent in a single session — the steepest single-day loss of any primary holding — and analysts are already calling it a buying opportunity. The memory boom narrative just got a severe stress test.

The broader market sold off sharply on Tuesday, with the Nasdaq composite falling on renewed geopolitical pressure tied to escalating tensions between the United States and Iran, including reports of a Trump administration blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. Oil prices spiked on the news, hitting energy-sensitive and high-beta growth names across the board. No specific index closing figures were available in the data provided, but the macro tone was risk-off across technology and industrials.

Sandisk was Tuesday's top story, and the numbers demand attention. The stock closed at one thousand six hundred seventy-three dollars and ninety-seven cents, down two hundred forty-one dollars and ninety-five cents — a decline of twelve point six three percent. The move snapped a three-day winning streak and came as memory sector peers including Micron and SK Hynix also fell sharply amid the broader geopolitical selloff. What makes Tuesday's move notable is the analyst response: according to reporting from finance dot yahoo dot com, Wall Street raised price targets even as the stock sank, citing tight AI-driven memory supply as a structural tailwind. The bull case here rests on the memory boom outlasting near-term macro turbulence. For holders, the divergence between price action and analyst conviction is the signal worth tracking closely.

Applied Optoelectronics closed at one hundred eleven dollars and eighty-eight cents, down six point seven zero percent on the session. No company-specific news crossed the wire on Tuesday. The move tracks broadly with the technology sector selloff driven by macro pressure, and the stock remains the portfolio's largest position. Watch for any optical networking demand signals tied to AI infrastructure spending in the sessions ahead.

Redwire Corporation closed at nine dollars and fifty-nine cents, down five point eight zero percent. No direct news was available for Tuesday's session. The stock moved in sympathy with broader industrials weakness. Space sector sentiment was pressured across the board, which likely weighed on Redwire as well. Upcoming contract announcements or government program updates remain the key catalyst to monitor.

AST SpaceMobile closed at sixty-seven dollars and fifty-eight cents, down seven point eight three percent. Two forces drove that move. First, oil price spikes from the Strait of Hormuz situation pressured the broader space sector. Second, a surprise milestone from China's space program rattled the narrative around American rocket dominance, according to finance dot yahoo dot com. On the other side, retail trader sentiment remained supportive — AST SpaceMobile tied with SpaceX as the top beaten-down space pick among retail buyers, each capturing thirty-three percent of buying interest in a survey cited by finance dot yahoo dot com.

Nebius Group closed at two hundred ten dollars and fifty-one cents, down four point one six percent. Of note on Tuesday, reporting from finance dot yahoo dot com highlighted Google pushing its tensor processing units deeper into neocloud providers — a segment directly relevant to Nebius Group's positioning in the AI infrastructure stack. That competitive dynamic is worth watching as the neocloud buildout continues.

Bloom Energy closed at two hundred thirty-three dollars and forty-nine cents, down four point five five percent. Despite Tuesday's pullback, the fundamental narrative held firm. Finance dot yahoo dot com reported that ClearBridge Investments highlighted Bloom Energy in its second-quarter commentary, citing alternative power demand driven by AI infrastructure buildout. A separate piece described Bloom Energy as, quote, AI's emergency power button. The stock has gained twelve percent over the past three months according to additional reporting, and the longer-term demand case appears intact.

TeraWulf closed at twenty dollars and eighty-nine cents, down four point nine two percent. Finance dot yahoo dot com ran a comparative piece weighing TeraWulf against Applied Digital as competing neocloud infrastructure plays. No company-specific catalyst drove the move — this was macro-driven selling across the bitcoin mining and AI compute space. The neocloud positioning thesis remains the core watchpoint.

Lumentum Holdings closed at seven hundred sixty-eight dollars and fifteen cents, down four point two two percent. No company-specific news was available for Tuesday. The stock moved lower in line with the broader optical technology peer group. With Applied Optoelectronics also under pressure, optical networking names as a cohort felt the weight of the risk-off session.

IREN Limited closed at thirty-eight dollars and ninety-eight cents, down five point two five percent. No direct company news crossed on Tuesday. The move aligns with sector-wide pressure on technology and crypto-adjacent infrastructure names. Upcoming network capacity updates or mining economics data would be the next meaningful signal.

Three items to track over the next forty-eight hours. First, any Federal Reserve speaker appearances that could shift rate expectations and directly reprice high-growth technology names across this portfolio. Second, follow-on analyst commentary on Sandisk — multiple price target revisions were flagged Tuesday evening, and further upgrades or downgrades could move the stock materially. Third, developments in the Strait of Hormuz situation — further escalation would sustain oil price pressure on space and industrials names including AST SpaceMobile and Redwire.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch the Sandisk analyst response closely — if Wall Street's conviction holds, that twelve point six three percent drop may define the week.
